draft.introduction: Eusebius’s Vita Constantini (c. 337–339 CE) is a panegyric biography of the first Christian emperor. It presents Constantine’s reign as the fulfillment of divine providence and the dawn of a Christian empire.

translation.text: He said that about midday, when the sun was beginning to decline, he saw with his own eyes the sign of a cross of light in the heavens, above the sun, bearing the inscription: “By this, conquer.” He was struck with amazement, and his whole army also witnessed the miracle.
translation.original_text: ἀμφὶ μεσημβρινὰς ἡλίου ὥρας, σταυροῦ τρόπαιον ἐκ φωτὸς ἐν οὐρανῷ ὑπερκείμενον τοῦ ἡλίου ἰδεῖν ἔφη, γραφῆς αὐτῷ συνημμένης· τούτῳ νίκα

translator_notes.note: The vision of the cross with the inscription “by this, conquer” (τούτῳ νίκα, Latin: in hoc signo vinces) is the founding myth of Christian imperial power. Eusebius’s account is hagiographic rather than historical, but the story reshaped the relationship between Christianity and political authority.
